theme: The Amen Break: The Six-Second Loop That Changed Music
description: One drum break, from one forgotten B-side, run through five completely different genres. Same six seconds of drumming, five different lives.
mood_arc: Traces the break from its hip-hop roots into jungle/drum and bass intensity, then out into unexpected corners of rock and pop -- same source, wildly different destinations.

tracks.title: Straight Outta Compton
tracks.youtube_query: NWA Straight Outta Compton official audio
tracks.pre_announce: Before we get into this, one piece of background: there's a drum break from a 1969 B-side called Amen, Brother by a soul group called The Winstons -- six seconds long, never a hit at the time. It's since become one of the most sampled pieces of music in history. This track is one of the early, well-known examples of it turning up in hip-hop.
tracks.back_announce: That break -- chopped up, sped around -- is part of a lineage that gets cited constantly in Amen break histories. The Winstons themselves reportedly never saw royalties from any of it; the break was lifted for decades before sample clearance was standard practice.
tracks.connection_to_next: Hip-hop is where the break got famous. But it's jungle and drum and bass, a few years later in the UK, where producers really took it apart and rebuilt it -- speeding it up until it barely sounds like a drum kit anymore.
facts: The Amen break originates from The Winstons' 1969 single 'Amen, Brother,' the B-side to 'Color Him Father.'
The Winstons' drummer, G.C. Coleman, played the break and reportedly never received royalties for its use.

tracks.title: Blood Sugar
tracks.pre_announce: This is what the Amen break sounds like once drum and bass producers got hold of it and pushed the tempo somewhere hip-hop never went.
tracks.back_announce: Blood Sugar -- one of the most straightforwardly Amen-break-driven tracks in the drum and bass canon, chopped and layered at well over double the tempo of the original 1969 recording.
tracks.connection_to_next: That's the break at its most recognisable and aggressive. Now here's the same idea landing somewhere nobody would expect it: mid-90s art rock.
facts: Drum and bass as a genre leans on the Amen break so heavily that entire sample packs exist purely of pre-chopped Amen variations.
Pendulum crossed over from drum and bass into a much bigger rock-festival audience through tracks built on breaks like this one.

tracks.title: Little Wonder
tracks.youtube_query: David Bowie Little Wonder official audio
tracks.pre_announce: Bowie spent the mid-90s listening to jungle and drum and bass, and Earthling is the album where that shows up most directly.
tracks.back_announce: Little Wonder, from Earthling. The whole album leans on chopped, heavily processed breakbeats in the jungle tradition this set has been tracing -- Bowie was reportedly listening to a lot of Underworld and drum and bass radio at the time he made it.
tracks.connection_to_next: From art-rock's jungle phase to Britpop's, in the same era -- the next track opens with a break so recognisable it's become a genre in-joke.
facts: Earthling was released in 1997, during a period when Bowie was actively engaging with London's drum and bass scene.
Bowie has cited Trent Reznor and UK jungle/DnB producers as reference points for the album's sound.

tracks.title: D'You Know What I Mean?
tracks.youtube_query: Oasis D'You Know What I Mean official video
tracks.pre_announce: Same year as the last one, completely different scene -- and it opens with a drum loop that's long been pointed to as an Amen-break lift, in a Britpop context nobody expects it in.
tracks.back_announce: D'You Know What I Mean, off Be Here Now. That opening break sits underneath one of the most maximalist productions of the Britpop era -- a genuinely strange place to find the same six seconds that powered jungle raves and NWA records.
tracks.connection_to_next: That's the loop turning up somewhere gentle by comparison. This last one is the opposite -- one of the most aggressive, controversial uses of a breakbeat from the same year.
facts: Be Here Now (1997) was, at the time, one of the fastest-selling albums in UK chart history.
The album version of D'You Know What I Mean runs well over seven minutes; the single was trimmed down.

tracks.title: Smack My Bitch Up
tracks.youtube_query: The Prodigy Smack My Bitch Up official audio
tracks.pre_announce: Last one -- same year again, 1997 was clearly a big year for breakbeats crossing over. This is The Prodigy at their most confrontational.
tracks.back_announce: Smack My Bitch Up. The Prodigy came directly out of UK rave and breakbeat culture, and this track -- and its video -- became one of the most controversial and talked-about releases of the decade, for reasons that have nothing to do with the drums underneath it.
facts: The Fat of the Land (1997) topped the charts in multiple countries including the US and UK.
The song's title and the accompanying music video drew significant controversy and radio bans on release.
